ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301718 covers moussaka as a general category; the vegetable-only Turkish variant without meat is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FMM covers moussaka generically; the vegetable-only Turkish variant is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for moussaka or vegetable moussaka. None assigned.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of vegetable moussaka is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of layered baked eggplant, potato, tomato sauce, and melted cheese.

nutrient_source.title: Moussaka, vegetable, baked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with FAO/INFOODS Mediterranean dish data for eggplant-based casseroles
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA entry for Turkish vegetable moussaka exists. Values estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for baked eggplant (NDB 11210), cooked potato (NDB 11362), tomato sauce (NDB 11549), olive oil (NDB 04053), and part-skim mozzarella/hard cheese (NDB 01028), weighted to a representative recipe ratio (eggplant 35%, potato 25%, tomato sauce 20%, cheese 12%, olive oil 5%, onion/seasoning 3%). Energy cross-checked against published Mediterranean vegetable moussaka analyses in peer-reviewed literature (Trichopoulou et al., 2000, British Journal of Nutrition).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for eggplant-based mixed vegetable bake
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Turkish vegetable moussaka. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database analogous entries: baked eggplant dishes (GI ~35–40), potato-containing mixed vegetable casseroles (GI ~50–55), and tomato-sauce-based Mediterranean dishes (GI ~40–50). Weighted composite estimate accounting for eggplant (35%), potato (25%), tomato sauce (20%), and cheese (12%) proportions yields an estimated GI of approximately 45. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (45 × 9.8 × 3.0) / 100 = 13.2 g. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~280 mg/100 g) from cheese, tomato sauce, and added seasonings
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size as a 300 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 840 mg sodium (~37% of the 2,300 mg daily limit); requesting reduced-salt preparation or limiting portion to 150 g can help manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low estimated glycemic index (~45) driven by high fibre eggplant and moderate carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Vegetable moussaka has a low estimated GI (~45) and moderate carbohydrate content (~9.8 g/100 g), making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions (300 g), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 13 g per serving; pairing with a green salad further attenuates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil with moderate saturated fat from cheese
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The use of olive oil as the primary cooking fat provides cardioprotective monounsaturated fatty acids (~2.6 g/100 g), and the dish's vegetable-dominant composition supports a Mediterranean dietary pattern associated with reduced cardiovascular risk; saturated fat from cheese (1.8 g/100 g) remains within acceptable limits for most cardiovascular guidelines when consumed in standard portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Moussaka (Turkish: Sebze Musakka) is a traditional Turkish baked casserole dish made without meat, distinguishing it from the Greek meat-based moussaka. The dish is prepared by slicing eggplants (aubergines) and lightly pan-frying them in olive oil until golden, then layering them in a deep casserole dish with sliced potatoes, sautéed onions, tomato sauce, and aromatic seasonings such as black pepper, allspice, and dried herbs. Grated hard cheese is scattered over the top before the dish is baked in the oven until the cheese melts and the vegetables are fully tender. The result is a hearty, flavourful casserole with a soft, yielding texture from the eggplant and potato, a slightly tangy tomato base, and a savoury, lightly browned cheese crust. Nutritionally, vegetable moussaka is a moderate-energy dish (~98 kcal/100 g) with a favourable macronutrient profile: relatively low in carbohydrates (~9.8 g/100 g), moderate in protein (~3.8 g/100 g) from cheese, and moderate in fat (~5.2 g/100 g) dominated by heart-healthy monounsaturated fats from olive oil. The dish provides meaningful dietary fibre (~2.4 g/100 g) from eggplant and potato, supporting satiety and glycaemic control. Its estimated glycemic index of approximately 45 places it in the low-GI category, making it appropriate for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Sodium content (~280 mg/100 g) warrants attention for individuals with hypertension. The dish is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, and is commonly served as a main course in Turkish and Levantine restaurants. In Singapore, it is offered at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine at Boat Quay.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Turkish vegetable moussaka was found in any primary approved source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data for the constituent ingredients (eggplant, potato, tomato sauce, olive oil, cheese) weighted to a representative recipe ratio, cross-referenced with published Mediterranean dish analyses. GI was estimated analogously from University of Sydney GI Database entries for comparable eggplant-based and potato-containing Mediterranean casseroles. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not derived from a direct laboratory analysis of this dish. Variation across restaurant preparations is expected. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 3. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g/100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for the constituent ingredients (olive oil, vegetables, cheese) — no industrial trans fats are present. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Turkish vegetable moussaka was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 6. The dish is served at Sultan Palace Turkish and Lebanese Cuisine (Boat Quay, Singapore) at SGD 19.90 per portion. 7. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes use closest_parent match as no exact code for Turkish vegetable-only moussaka exists in either ontology. 8. The food_name has been normalised from the menu-style entry '20. Mousakka Vegetables' to the canonical English name 'Vegetable Moussaka'.
